pmos

Growth Accounting — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. Your product added 10,000 monthly active users last month. Good news — or is it? Maybe you won 60,000 new users and quietly lost 50,000 old ones. The net number, +10,000, hides that. Growth accounting is the method that takes it apart. It sorts every user into one of four buckets each period — new, retained, churned, and resurrected — and ties them together with a single bookkeeping identity, the way a balance sheet ties assets to liabilities. From those buckets fall a handful of diagnostics: the quick ratio (gains divided by losses), gross retention, and, for subscription businesses, net dollar retention. This primer is a deep, plain-English tour of that one framework, written for product managers: what it is and who built it; why a net number lies; the four states and the core identity; the quick ratio and how to read it; the revenue version; net dollar retention; the chart that makes it visible; the traps that fool people; and what to actually do with it. Every term of art is defined on first use and every acronym is spelled out. Source provenance: curation-consensus across the growth canon — Jonathan Hsu's Social Capital essays, Amplitude, Sequoia, Tribe Capital, Tomasz Tunguz, and the SaaS-metrics literature. 17 verified sources.

What growth accounting is

Growth accounting is a bookkeeping method for user and revenue growth. It borrows its spirit from financial accounting: rather than reporting one bottom-line number, it shows the gross flows that produced it. Amplitude describes it as breaking down overall growth into growth across specific user segments — splitting the change in your active-user count into the distinct groups of people who created it.

The framework was popularized by Jonathan Hsu, formerly a data scientist at Facebook and then a partner at the venture firm Social Capital, in a widely read 2017 essay series. Hsu's pitch was that startups deserve the same rigor public companies get from GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, the standard rulebook for financial reporting). In his epilogue he placed growth accounting inside a larger toolkit he nicknamed the 8-ball, which consists of the three pieces ... 1) growth accounting, 2) cohort behavior and 3) distribution of product-market fit, each measured for the core unit of value — users, revenue, or both.

The promise is diagnostic, not cosmetic. Sequoia frames the same idea as splitting growth into new users, resurrected users, retained users and churned users so you can see which flow is driving the headline. A primer-shaped way to hold it: growth accounting answers "where did this month's growth come from, and where did it leak out?"

Why net growth hides the story

A single net number — "monthly active users grew by 10,000" — is a difference of two much larger flows, and the difference can look identical for very different businesses. One product might add 60,000 and lose 50,000. Another might add 12,000 and lose 2,000. Both report +10,000. They are not the same company: the first is filling a leaky bucket, the second is barely leaking at all.

This is the core motivation for the whole framework. PM Curve, a product-analytics newsletter, frames growth accounting as an early-warning system — using growth rate and 3 metrics of growth accounting, we can quickly figure out whether there is any problem with current growth and fix it in advance, rather than seeing the dip in growth later when it's too late. The classic failure mode it describes: a company posts healthy top-line growth while retention is quietly rotting underneath, because aggressive acquisition keeps refilling the bucket faster than churn empties it — until acquisition slows and the floor falls out.

Monthly active users (MAU) is the count of distinct users who took a meaningful action in the last 30 days; the same logic applies to daily active users (DAU) and weekly active users (WAU). Whichever window you pick, growth accounting's job is to refuse the comforting average and force the gross flows into the open.

The four user states

Growth accounting begins by assigning every active user, each period, to exactly one of four mutually exclusive states. Amplitude defines them cleanly. New users are those who were first active in the present month — first-timers. Retained users were carried over from the previous period: active then, active again now. Resurrected users had churned at some point in the past but resumed — they came back from the dead, hence the name. And a churned user is one who was active in the previous month but has no activity in the present one.

Hsu's original definitions match: new are those acquiring the product for the first time, retained are those active in consecutive periods, churned are those who were active previously but are no longer, and resurrected are those returning after periods of inactivity. "Mutually exclusive" matters: in any given period a user is counted in one bucket only, so the buckets can be summed without double-counting.

Notice the symmetry. New and resurrected are the two ways your active-user count gains a person; churned is the one way it loses one; retained is the steady base that simply persists. That symmetry is what the next section turns into an equation.

The core identity

The four states are tied together by one equation — the identity that makes this "accounting" rather than a loose set of metrics. Hsu states it as the change in active users between two periods: MAU(t) - MAU(t - 1 month) = new(t) + resurrected(t) - churned(t). In words: this month's active-user count minus last month's equals the people you gained (new plus resurrected) minus the people you lost (churned).

Retained users do not appear in that difference — and that is the point. They are in both months, so they cancel out of the change. They still matter enormously (they are most of your business), but the movement in the headline number is driven entirely by gains and losses at the edges. Sequoia writes the same relationship as Δ growth = new users + resurrected users - churned users, where Δ (the Greek letter delta) just means "the change in."

There is a companion identity worth knowing. Ben Bregman's walkthrough notes that your current active-user count can itself be decomposed: at any point the current [x]AU of your app at any point can be described as retained + new + resurrected users. So one identity describes the level (retained + new + resurrected = total active) and the other describes the change (new + resurrected − churned = month-over-month delta). Together they leave no flow unaccounted for, which is exactly what "accounting" should guarantee.

Retention in growth accounting

The buckets give you retention almost for free. Gross retention is the share of last period's active users who stayed active this period — that is, retained users divided by the prior period's total active users. It answers "of everyone I had, how many did I keep?" and it can never exceed 100 percent, because you cannot keep more people than you started with.

Hold gross retention next to its mirror, churn. If you kept 92 percent, you lost 8 percent; the two always sum to 100 percent of the prior base. PM Curve packages growth accounting's read into three diagnostic metrics — gross retention, the quick ratio, and net churn — and argues this trio is what surfaces a problem early, because it can quickly figure out whether there is any problem with current growth and fix it in advance. Gross retention is the "are we holding the base?" gauge; the quick ratio, next, is the "are gains beating losses?" gauge.

One subtlety: gross retention is about users, not revenue. A business can retain most of its users while losing revenue if those users downgrade — which is exactly why the subscription world adds a separate revenue-retention measure later in this primer. For now, the mental model is: retention measures the durability of the base; the quick ratio measures the vigor at the edges.

The quick ratio

The quick ratio compresses all four states into a single health number. It is the ratio of users you added to users you lost. Hsu defines it as Quick Ratio = (new + resurrected)/churned, and notes it must exceed 1.0 for growth. TheVentureCity puts it the same way: the ratio of users added over users lost is called the Quick Ratio, and if a Quick Ratio is >1 the number of users is growing; conversely, if it is <1 the number of users is declining.

Its appeal is that one number blends four behaviors. Stefano Maglietta, a practitioner who has written on the metric, calls it a comprehensive measure precisely because elements of user acquisition, retention, churning and re-activation (resurrection) are blended into its calculation, so its trend can quickly assess the health of the business. Watching one line over time is cheaper than watching four.

Read it as a ratio of momentum: a quick ratio of 2 means that for every user you lost, you added two. Exactly 1 means gains and losses are balanced and your active count is flat. Below 1 means you are shrinking no matter how good the headline acquisition number looks — because losses are outpacing gains.

Reading the quick ratio

What counts as a good quick ratio depends on whether you are measuring users or revenue, and on your business model. For the revenue version (defined fully in the next section), the SaaS-metrics literature has settled on a widely cited target. Wall Street Prep calls 4.0 ... the most frequently cited target benchmark, and makes it concrete: a 4.0 means for every $4.00 in revenue generated, only $1.00 is lost from churn or contraction. (SaaS stands for software as a service — software sold by ongoing subscription rather than a one-time license.)

Cobloom offers a three-tier reading that is easy to remember. Below 1, You're losing revenue from churn faster than you can replace it; between 1 and 4, growth is real but inefficient; and above 4 is what it calls The "optimum" Quick Ratio. It cites analytics firm InsightSquared's finding that the fastest-growing SaaS companies averaged a quick ratio of about 3.9 — close to that 4.0 rule of thumb.

Two cautions before you anchor on any benchmark. First, the famous "4" is a revenue target; for a free consumer product measured in users, even a ratio comfortably above 1 can be healthy. Second — and this is the theme of the limitations section — a single ratio can hide a lot. Tomasz Tunguz, a venture investor who writes prolifically on SaaS metrics, warns bluntly that high growth rates can mask high dollar churn rates, so a flattering ratio is a starting question, not a final answer.

Revenue growth accounting

Everything so far counted people. The same machinery works on dollars, with one important addition. When Hsu extended the framework to revenue, he had to add two states that have no equivalent in the user version: expansion and contraction. As he explains, a single customer may be retained or churned but they may also be retained as a customer by spending more or less in the second period. A user is binary — active or not — but a customer's spend can rise (expansion) or fall (contraction) while they stay.

Hsu also flags a deep difference in the underlying economics. Subscriptions behave opposite to apps: Recurring subscription revenue is default-retained in contrast with recurring visitation which is default-not-retained. A subscriber keeps paying unless they actively cancel, whereas an app user must actively return each period or they lapse. That asymmetry is why he holds subscription businesses to a higher bar, preferring companies with a quick ratio greater than 4.

The revenue quick ratio formula follows directly. Geckoboard writes it as New MRR + Expansion MRR / Churned MRR + Contraction MRR, and Wall Street Prep gives the same with parentheses: SaaS Quick Ratio = (New MRR + Expansion MRR) ÷ (Churned MRR + Contraction MRR). Here MRR is monthly recurring revenue — the predictable subscription revenue booked each month; its annual cousin is ARR, annual recurring revenue. The structure is identical to the user version: gains on top, losses on the bottom, with expansion joining new as a gain and contraction joining churn as a loss.

Net dollar retention

Revenue growth accounting leads naturally to the metric many subscription investors now watch most closely: net dollar retention (NDR), also called net revenue retention (NRR). It measures how much recurring revenue you keep from your existing customers over a period, once upgrades, downgrades, and cancellations are netted together. Stripe defines it as measuring how well a business retains its existing revenue from current customers, accounting for upgrades, downgrades, and churn.

The formula nets the same flows you already know. Stripe writes NDR = ((Starting MRR + Expansion − Contraction − Churn) ÷ Starting MRR) × 100%; the Corporate Finance Institute gives the same identity as [(Starting MRR + Expansion MRR – Contraction – Churned MRR) / Starting MRR] × 100. Critically, it excludes brand-new customers — it isolates what is happening inside the base you already had.

The number to remember is 100 percent. Above it, your existing customers are growing your revenue all by themselves. Stripe notes an NDR greater than 100% indicates that revenue from existing customers is growing; the Corporate Finance Institute agrees that NDR above 100% means that monthly recurring revenue has grown due to expansion revenue outweighing any losses. That is the prized "negative net churn" state: expansion from upgrades more than covers everything lost to downgrades and cancellations. Per the OpenView benchmarks Stripe cites, the strongest midmarket and enterprise companies reach roughly 125 percent at the high end — a base that compounds before a single new logo is added.

The growth accounting chart

Growth accounting is most useful as a picture. The canonical chart stacks the gains above a zero line and the losses below it, period by period, so the net change is the visible gap between them. Tribe Capital, a firm built around this style of analysis, describes a two-panel view: one panel showing gains (new, resurrected, and expansion) and losses (churned and contraction), and a companion panel tracking gross retention, quick ratio, and net churn over time.

Ben Bregman explains why the chart is worth building: because current active users decompose into retained plus new plus resurrected, a growth-accounting chart immediately provides explanation for the current [x]AU and any deltas over time. You stop arguing about whether last month was good and start seeing which bar grew or shrank.

Tribe Capital goes further and treats the whole picture as a product-market-fit gauge — growth accounting breaks down overall growth in some activity across specific customer segments, and reading the shape over time tells you where on the spectrum a product sits. It stresses that product-market fit is a spectrum, not a yes/no verdict — and the chart is how you locate yourself on it. (PMF, product-market fit, is the point at which a product satisfies a strong market demand.)

Limitations and pitfalls

Growth accounting is honest about flows, but it can still be misread, and three traps recur. The first is trusting the quick ratio alone. Tomasz Tunguz titles an entire piece around the question of what the ratio hides, and shows that high growth rates can mask high dollar churn rates — a company can post a quick ratio of 4 while its underlying churn is, in his word, unsustainably high, because furious new-revenue growth temporarily outruns the leak. His prescription is to always read the ratio alongside the absolute churn rate.

The second trap is the time window. The definitions of "churned" and "resurrected" depend entirely on the period length you choose, and that choice has consequences. Marton Trencseni's writeup spells out the trade-off: DAU and WAU respond quicker to problems (with MAU, if there's a problem, people won't enter Churned for 28 days after the last activity), but are also more volatile. A monthly window is stable but slow to flag trouble; a daily window is sensitive but noisy. There is no universally right window — only one matched to how often your product is meant to be used.

The third trap is the one the whole framework exists to defeat, and it still catches people who look only at the top line: strong acquisition masking weak retention. PM Curve's case study describes exactly this pattern — a company whose headline growth looked fine while the growth-accounting breakdown revealed deteriorating retention underneath, the kind of rot that only surfaces in the net number once acquisition cools. The lesson is consistent across all three: never read one growth-accounting number in isolation.

What product managers should know

For a product manager, growth accounting is less a dashboard to admire than a discipline for asking better questions. The first habit: every time someone cites a net growth number, ask for the four flows behind it. A +10,000 built on 60,000 new and 50,000 churned demands a retention investigation; the same +10,000 on 12,000 new and 2,000 churned is a healthy machine you should pour fuel on. The framework, as PM Curve puts it, exists so teams can fix it in advance, rather than seeing the dip in growth later when it's too late.

The second habit: match the metric to the lifecycle stage. Sequoia observes that the mix shifts as a product matures — early on, resurrection and churn swing wildly, but at scale resurrection and churn roughly cancel each other out and much of the net growth happens through new user growth. A young product should obsess over the quick ratio and resurrection; a mature one should watch the durability of its base and, if it is a subscription business, its net dollar retention.

The third habit: pick the unit of value deliberately. Hsu's whole point in the 8-ball essay is that growth accounting should be run on the core unit of value for the business (typically users and/or (possibly recurring) revenue). A media product accounts in active users; a SaaS product accounts in MRR; many do both, because the user picture and the revenue picture can diverge — and the gap between them is itself a signal worth chasing.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Growth accounting sits at the center of a cluster of product-analytics methods. These are pointers, not lessons — each is its own study.

  • Cohort analysis — growth accounting tells you the net flows this period; cohort analysis follows a single start-group as it ages, which is how you diagnose why churn and resurrection move. Hsu's 8-ball pairs the two deliberately.
  • Retention curves and the retention table — the visual backbone beneath gross retention; a flattening curve is the cohort-level signal of product-market fit.
  • Customer lifetime value (LTV) and unit economics — retention and net dollar retention feed directly into how much a customer is worth over their lifetime, and whether acquisition pays back.
  • North-star metrics and the active-user definition — every number here depends on what counts as "active"; choosing that definition is a prior decision growth accounting inherits.
  • SaaS metrics (MRR, ARR, churn, CAC payback) — the revenue version of growth accounting is one corner of the broader subscription-metrics toolkit.
pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_growth-accounting.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
